content.problemStatement:
Let's call a sequence with length KKK good if there are at most K2+1\frac{K}{2} + 12K‚Äã+1 distinct elements in it. For example, the sequence (3,3,2,1,3)(3, 3, 2, 1, 3)(3,3,2,1,3) is good, since it contains 3‚â§52+13 \le \frac{5}{2} + 13‚â§25‚Äã+1 distinct elements, while the sequence (1,4,8,8,2)(1, 4, 8, 8, 2)(1,4,8,8,2) is not good, since it contains 4>52+14 \gt \frac{5}{2} + 14>25‚Äã+1 distinct elements.

You are given a sequence of integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã. You may perform any number of operations (including zero) on this sequence. In one operation, you should choose one element of the sequence and replace it by any integer you choose. You want all contiguous subsequences of the resulting sequence to be good. What is the smallest number of operations you need to perform to achieve this?
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5‚ãÖ1055 \cdot 10^55‚ãÖ105
Subtasks**Subtask #1 (20 points):** the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 200200200
**Subtask #2 (30 points):** the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5,0005,0005,000
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ‚Äï the smallest number of operations needed to make all contiguous subsequences good.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: All contiguous subsequences are already good.
Example case 2: For example, (1,100,10000)(1, 100, 10000)(1,100,10000) is not good. It is enough to change the sequence to (1,100,100,1000000)(1, 100, 100, 1000000)(1,100,100,1000000), though.
Example case 3: For example, (1,2,3)(1, 2, 3)(1,2,3) is not good. It is enough to change the sequence to (1,2,1,2,1)(1, 2, 1, 2, 1)(1,2,1,2,1).
